title: PRESENTATION XII BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR AN OWN MORPHISM

pages.title: PRESENTATION XII BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR AN OWN MORPHISM

blocks.html:
PRESENTATION XII
BASE CHANGE THEOREM FOR AN OWN MORPHISMby M. Artin
Summary

1. Introduction
2. An example
3. Reminders on the non-abelian \(H^1\)
4. The base change morphism
5. Statement of the main theorem and some variants
6. First reductions
7. A variation of Chow's Lemma
8. Permanent reductions
1. Introduction

We give in this presentation the fundamental theorem which says, under a slightly more precise form, than for a proper morphism \(f : X \to Y\) of diagrams, and for an abelian beam \(F\) of torsion on \(X\), the fiber of \(R^q f_* F\) at a geometric point of \(Y\) is isomorphic to the cohomology \(H^q(X_{\bar y}, F|X_{\bar y})\) of fiber \(X_{\bar y}\).

Recall that for a proper morphism of paracompact spaces the analogous result is well known (GODEMENT II 4.11) and easy. He is much more delicate in the case of diagrams. For example, the hypothesis that \(F\) is twisted is very necessary, contrary to what is happening for paracompact spaces.

The demonstration is done in several stages: we reduce ourselves by more or less formal unscrewing methods in the case where \(Y\) is Noetherian, where the relative dimension of \(f\) is \(\leq 1\), and where the beam \(F\) is constant. For treat this particular case, we use the specialization theorem for the fundamental group (5.9) which is a non-abelian variant of the theorem stated above, and which can be directly demonstrated in the particular case- considered (XIII 2).
2. An example

Let us show that the hypothesis that \(F\) is torsion is necessary: Let \(X\) be a non-singular surface on an algebraically closed body \(k\), \(Y\) a non-singular curve on \(k\), and \(f : X \to Y\) a proper morphism whose the generic fiber is smooth and the geometric fiber at the geometric point \(\bar y\) of \(Y\) has an ordinary double point, and is irreducible. Let \(F\) be the beam constant \(\mathbb{Z}_X\).

Let us recall the following fact: for any point \(z\) of a diagram \(Z\), or \(i : z \to Z\) the inclusion. We have \(H^1(Z, i_* \mathbb{Z}_z) = 0\) (IX 3.6 (i)). Gold since \(X\) is normal and therefore unibranched, it follows that \(\mathbb{Z}_X \simeq i_* \mathbb{Z}_x\), where \(i : x \to X\) is the generic point inclusion. So \(H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}_X) = 0\) and even \(R^1 f_* \mathbb{Z}_X = 0\). But I say that for the geometric fiber \(X_o\) of \(X/Y\) with the ordinary double point we have \(H^1(X_o, \mathbb{Z}_{X_o}) \simeq \mathbb{Z}\) (*), which gives the counter- example, since \(\mathbb{Z}_{X_o} \simeq \mathbb{Z}_X|X_o\).

Let \(i_o : x_o \to X_o\) be the inclusion of the generic point of \(X_o\), and let \(Q\) the singular point of \(X_o\). The curve \(X_o\) has two "branches" at \(Q\) and it follows that the fiber from \(i_{o*}(\mathbb{Z}_{x_o})\) at the point \(Q\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}\). Outside of \(Q\) the curve \(X_o\) is normal and therefore \(i_{o*}(\mathbb{Z}_{x_o}) \simeq \mathbb{Z}_{X_o}\) in \(X_o - Q\). We therefore have a exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}_{X_o} \longrightarrow i_{o*}(\mathbb{Z}_{x_o}) \longrightarrow (\mathbb{Z})_Q \longrightarrow 0 , \] where the last member is the extension by \(0\) of the sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}\) at the point \(Q\). All all three members have a \(H^0\) isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}\), and \(H^1(X_o, i_{o*}(\mathbb{Z}_{x_o})) = 0\), hence \(H^1(X_o, \mathbb{Z}_{X_o}) = \mathbb{Z}\) by the exact cohomology sequence.

footnotes.html: (*) Compare, from the point of view \(\pi_1\), with SGA 3 X (6, Examples) and (1.6).

blocks.html: 3. Reminders on the non-abelian \(H^1\)

We will briefly indicate some results on cohomology non-abelian which we will need in the following. We will omit the most of the demonstrations, well known in various particular cases, and which can be found in GIRAUD's thesis [1].

Let \(X\) be a schema, and \(F\) be a group bundle on \(X\). For each \(X'/X\) spreads, we define a pointed set \(H^1(X', F)\), functorial covariant in \(F\) and contravariant to \(X'\). \(H^1\) switches to end beam products. If \(f : X \to Y\) is a morphism, we define a sheaf of pointed sets (i.e., with distinguished section) \(R^1 f_* F\). This is the beam associated with pre-bundle (of pointed sets) \(\mathcal{R}^1 F\), which, to each \(Y'/Y\) spreads, associates the set \(H^1(X \times_Y Y', F)\).
We have the usual exact cohomology sequences, from which we we will just indicate one:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.1

Let \(0 \to F \xrightarrow{\ u\ } G\) be an injection of group bundles, and let \(C = G/F\) be the sheaf of homogeneous sets under \(G\). We have a sequel exact beams of pointed sets \[ 0 \longrightarrow f_* F \longrightarrow f_* G \longrightarrow f_* C \xrightarrow{\ \partial\ } R^1 f_* F \xrightarrow{\ u^1\ } R^1 f_* G . \]

Of course, this exact sequence is obtained by passing to the sequence of beams associated with the exact sequence of pre-beams whose value for \(Y'/Y\) calm is \[ 0 \longrightarrow F(X') \longrightarrow G(X') \longrightarrow C(X') \xrightarrow{\ \partial\ } H^1(X', F) \longrightarrow H^1(X', G) , \] where \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\). The equivalence relation induced by \(\partial\) is the one given by the operation of \(f_* G\) on \(f_* C\). As usual, we can explain the equivalence relation induced by \(u^1\) thanks to the notion of "torsion" [1].

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

Let \(X \xrightarrow{\ f\ } Y \xrightarrow{\ g\ } Z\) be schema morphisms. We have an exact sequence for each \(Z'/Z\) spread, setting \(Y' = Y \times_Z Z'\), \(X' = X \times_Z Z'\): \[ 0 \longrightarrow H^1(Y', f_* F) \longrightarrow H^1(X', F) \longrightarrow H^0(Y', R^1 f_* F) , \] hence, passing to the associated beams, an exact sequence of pointed beams on \(Z\): \[ 0 \longrightarrow (R^1 g_*) f_* F \longrightarrow R^1 (gf)_* F \longrightarrow g_* (R^1 f_*) F . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.3

The family of functors of the form \(H^1(X', F)\), for \(X'\) spreads on \(X\), and of the form \(R^1 f_* F\), for any morphism \(f : X \to Y\), is erasable, i.e. there exists an injection \(F \xrightarrow{\ u\ } G\) such that the morphisms induced \(H^1(X', F) \to H^1(X', G)\) and \(R^1 f_* F \to R^1 f_* G\) are all zero. More precisely, there exists an injection \(F \xrightarrow{\ u\ } G\) such that each of the sets \(H^1(X', G)\), with \(X'\) spread over \(X\), and each of the pointed beams \(R^1 f_* G\), are null. If \(X\) is Noetherian and \(F\) is a sheaf of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-construct groups- tible, then there exists such an injection, where further \(G\) is a bundle of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups.

Demonstration. The first assertion is undoubtedly true in a topos any, but for the flat topology, it is convenient to erase cement with the "Godement resolution" \[ (3.4) \qquad F \longrightarrow G = \prod_{x \in X} i_{x*} i_x^* F , \] where \(x\) traverses the set of points of \(X\) and where \(i_x : \bar x \to X\) is a point geometry of \(X\) above \(x\). Note that for \(X'/X\) spread, we have \[ G(X') = \prod_{x \in X} (i_x^* F)(X' \times_X \bar x) = \prod_{x \in X} \Big( \prod_{\bar x'/\bar x} F_{\bar x'} \Big) , \] where the \(\bar x'\) are geometric points of \(X'\). But it is clear that for every point of \(X'\) there is at least one \(\bar x'\) above it, hence \(F(X') \subset G(X')\) (VIII 3), therefore \(F \subset G\). On the other hand, any \(\bar u / \bar x\) equator is sum of spectra of separably closed bodies, hence \(H^1(\bar u, \bar G) = 0\), whatever beam \(\bar G\) to \(\bar x\). It then follows from 3.2 that \(H^1(X', i_{x*} i_x^* F) = 0\) for each \(X'/X\) spreads, hence (since \(H^1\) commutes to the products) \(H^1(X', G) = 0\), therefore the same \(R^1 f_* G = 0\) for all \(f : X \to Y\), thanks to the description of \(R^1 f_*(G)\) as the beam associated with pre-beam \(Y' \mapsto H^1(X \times_Y Y', G)\).

Now suppose that further \(F\) is constructible and \(X\) noethé- nothing. Then we immediately see that there exists a finite set of points \(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}\) of \(X\) such that the morphism \[ F \longrightarrow \prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} i_{x_i*} i_{x_i}^* F \] is already injective. The sheaf \(i_{x*} i_x^* F\) is a sheaf of ind-finite groups (IX 1.5), therefore the finished product is also (IX 1.4 and 1.6 (iii)). In replacement placing \(G\) by this beam, we obtain the last assertion.
3.5. As in the case of Abelian cohomology (treated in V 2.4), we can calculate the \(H^1\) by the ČECH method [1].
4. The base change morphism

Either \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g'} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]_{g} } \] a Cartesian square of morphism of diagrams. We deduce a morphism of functor \(\varphi = \varphi_g\) \[ (4.1) \qquad g^* f_* \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } f'_* g'^* \] in the following way: giving such a morphism is equivalent to giving a mor- phism of functors \[ (4.1\ \mathrm{adj}) \qquad f_* \longrightarrow g_* f'_* g'^* , \] because \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) are adjoint. We take for this morphism the morphism \[ f_* \xrightarrow{\ (\mathrm{id} \to g'_* g'^*)\ } f_* g'_* g'^* = g_* f'_* g'^* , \] taking into account that \[ g_* f'_* = (gf')_* = (fg')_* = f_* g'_* . \] Note that there is another candidate for (4.1). In fact, giving such a morphism is equivalent to giving \[ f'^* g^* f_* \longrightarrow g'^* \] and we can take \[ f'^* g^* f_* \simeq g'^* f^* f_* \xrightarrow{\ (f^* f_* \to \mathrm{id})\ } g'^* . \]
I don't know if these two choices always coincide (*), and in any case we will only use the first. We call it morphism of change of base.

By restricting (4.1) to abelian sheaves (resp. groups) we deduce a morphism \(\varphi^q = \varphi^q_g\) for each \(q \geq 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0, 1\)): \[ (4.2) \qquad g^*(R^q f_*) \xrightarrow{\ \varphi^q\ } (R^q f'_*) g'^* . \] In fact, it is the same as giving \[ (4.2\ \mathrm{adj}) \qquad (R^q f_*) \longrightarrow g_*(R^q f'_*) g'^* , \] and we have "obvious" morphisms \[ (R^q f_*) \xrightarrow{\ a\ } (R^q f_*) g'_* g'^* \xrightarrow{\ b\ } R^q (f g')_* g'^* = = R^q (gf')_* g'^* \xrightarrow{\ c\ } g^*(R^q f'_*) g'^* . \]

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.3

Suppose \(g\) (so \(g'\)) is a closed immersion. So the morphisms \(b\) and \(c\) above are isomorphisms (VIII 5.7). It follows that (4.2) is essentially none other than the morphism \(a\) above, which is defined by applying \(R^q f_*\) to the canonical morphism \(F \to g'_* g'^* F\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.4

(Composition of base change morphisms).

(i) Either \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g'} \ar[d]^{f'} & X'' \ar[l]_{h'} \ar[d]^{f''} \\ S & S' \ar[l]_{g} & S'' \ar[l]_{h} } \] a Cartesian diagram of schema morphisms. So the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ (gh)^* f_* \ar[rr]^{gh} \ar[d]_{\wr} & & f''_* (g'h')^* \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ h^* g^* f_* \ar[r]^{h^*_* g} & h^* f'_* g'^* \ar[r]^{h^*_* g'^*} & f''_* h'^* g'^* } \] and the diagram with \(f_*, f'_*, f''_*\) replaced by \(R^q f_*, R^q f'_*, R^q f''_*\) is too, in the natural definition domains of the functors in question (see no. 3).

(ii) Either \[ \xymatrix{ Y \ar[d]_{e} & Y' \ar[l]_{g''} \ar[d]^{e'} \\ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g'} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]_{g} } \] a Cartesian diagram of schema morphisms. So the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ g^*(fe)_* \ar[rr] \ar@{=}[d] & & (f'e')_* g''^* \ar@{=}[d] \\ g^* f_* e_* \ar[r] & f'_* g'^* e_* \ar[r] & f'_* e'_* g''^* , } \] where the three horizontal arrows come from the homomorphisms of change Baseline for \(fe\), \(f\) and \(e\) respectively.
In the abelian case, the base change morphisms for \(fe\) are induced by a morphism of spectral sequences
\[ \xymatrix{ E_2^{p,q} = g^*(R^p f_*)(R^q e_*) F \ar@{=>}[r] \ar[d]_{\varphi} & g^* R^{p+q}(fe)_* F \ar[d]^{\varphi} \\ (R^p f'_*)(R^q e'_*) g''^* F \ar@{=>}[r] & R^{p+q}(f'e')_* g'^* F } \]

which, for the initial terms, is induced by the change morphisms base for \(e\) and \(f\); and for a bundle of groups, we have a morphism of exact sequences \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & g^*(R^1 f_*) e_* F \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\varphi} & g^* R^1(fe)_* F \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\varphi} & g^* f_*(R^1 e_*) F \ar[d]_{\varphi} \\ 0 \ar[r] & (R^1 f'_*) e'_* g''^* F \ar[r] & R^1(f'e')_* g''^* F \ar[r] & f'_*(R^1 e'_*) g''^* F } \] We leave the demonstration to the reader.

footnotes.html: (*) Mr. DELIGNE resolved this perplexity in the affirmative, in a significantly more general context, cf. Exp. XVII.

pages.title: 5. Statement of the main theorem and some variants

blocks.html: 5. Statement of the main theorem and some variants
The theorem is as follows:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

(Base change theorem for a proper morphism). Either \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g'} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ S & S' \ar[l]_{g} } \] a Cartesian diagram of schemas, with \(f\) own.

  1. (i) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets over \(X\). Then the morphism (4.1) \[ \varphi : g^* f_* F \longrightarrow f'_* g'^* F \] is bijective.
  2. (ii) Let \(F\) be a sheaf of groups (resp. ind-finite groups) on \(X\). So the morphism (4.2) \[ \varphi^1 : g^*(R^1 f_*) F \longrightarrow (R^1 f'_*) g'^* F \] is injective (resp. bijective).
  3. (iii) Let \(F\) be an abelian torsion beam on \(X\). Then the morphism (4.2) \[ \varphi^q : g^*(R^q f_*) F \longrightarrow (R^q f'_*) g'^* F \] is bijective for each \(q \geq 0\).

We will first state the immediate consequences of the theorem. If \(S' = \xi\) is a geometric point of \(S\), so that a beam on \(\xi\) is determined mined by its global sections, we have \(H^0(\xi, R^q f'_* G) = H^q(X', G)\) for each do- ceau \(G\) on \(X\), hence the following corollary (which is also essentially equivalent to (5.1)):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(f : X \to S\) be proper, \(\xi \to S\) be a geometric point, and \(X_\xi\) the fiber of \(f\) at point \(\xi\).

  1. (i) If \(F\) is a sheaf of sets over \(X\), the canonical morphism of chan- basic accommodation \[ (f_* F)_\xi \longrightarrow H^0(X_\xi, F|X_\xi) \] is bijective.
  2. (ii) If \(F\) is a bundle of groups (resp. ind-finite groups) the mor- base change phism \[ R^1 f_*(F)_\xi \longrightarrow H^1(X_\xi, F|X_\xi) \] is injective (resp. bijective).
  3. (iii) If \(F\) is an abelian torsion beam, then for each \(q \geq 0\) the base change morphism \[ (R^q f_* F)_\xi \longrightarrow H^q(X_\xi, F|X_\xi) \] is bijective.
Taking into account (X 4.3) and (X 5.2), we find:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

Under the conditions of (5.2), let \(n\) be the dimension of the fiber \(X_\xi\). Then for each abelian torsion beam \(F\) on \(X\), the fiber \((R^q f_* F)_\xi\) is zero for \(q > 2n\). If \(F\) is a \(p\)-twist beam (\(p\) the characteristic of \(\xi\)), then the fiber of \(R^q f_*(F)\) in \(\xi\) is zero for \(q > n\).
Recall that we say that the relative dimension of \(X/S\) is \(\leq n\) if the dimension of each fiber is \(\leq n\). We therefore have

blocks.label: 5.3 bis
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3 bis

Let \(f : X \to S\) be proper and \(X/S\) of relative dimension \(\leq n\). Then \(R^q f_* F = 0\) if \(q > 2n\) for each abelian beam \(F\) of torsion on \(X\). If \(S\) has characteristic \(p\), and if \(F\) is an abelian sheaf of \(p\)-torsion, then \(R^q f_* F = 0\) if \(q > n\).
By applying (5.1) to a morphism of separably body spectra closed, we obtain

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

(Invariance of cohomology by change of base body in the proper case). Let \(k \subset K\) be separably closed fields and \(X\) be a schema clean on \(\operatorname{Spec} k\). Or \(X' = X_K\). Let \(F\) be a beam on \(X\) and \(F'\) be the beam reverse image to \(X'\). So

  1. (i) \[H^0(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^0(X', F') .\]
  2. (ii) If \(F\) is a bundle of groups (resp. ind-finite groups) \[ H^1(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^1(X', F') \] is injective (resp. bijective).
  3. (iii) If \(F\) is an abelian torsion beam, \[ H^q(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X', F') \] for everything \(q\).
A slightly different variation of (5.2) is as follows:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a Henselian ring and let \(S' = s_o\) be the closed point. Let \(f : X \to S\) be clean. Let's set \(X_o = X'\), \(F_o = g'^* F\). So we have:

  1. (i) \[H^0(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^0(X_o, F_o)\] for any bundle of sets \(F\).
  2. (ii) \[H^1(X, F) \longrightarrow H^1(X_o, F_o)\] is injective (resp. bijective) for any bundle of groups (resp. of groups ind-finis) \(F\).
  3. (iii) \[H^q(X, F) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X_o, F_o)\] for any abelian torsion beam \(F\), and all \(q\).

Indeed, this statement is only a special case of (5.2) if \(S\) is strictly local. Suppose (5.2) is known. Let \(\bar S\) be the strict localized of \(S\), \(\bar s\) the closed point of \(\bar S\), \(G\) the Galois group of \(\bar S/S\) (which is also the Galois group of \(\bar s/s_o\)). Then \(G\) operates on the fibers of \(R^q\), and we have (with obvious notations) \[ H^0(X, F) \simeq H^0(\bar X, \bar F)^G \] \[ H^0(X_o, F_o) \simeq H^0(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)^G \] for a sheaf of sets \(F\), hence (i) since \(H^0(\bar X, \bar F) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)\) and that the isomorphism obviously commutes with the operation of \(G\).

If \(F\) is a bundle of groups, we easily define a morphism of exact sequences of pointed sets \[ (5.5.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & H^1(G, H^0(\bar X, \bar F)) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^1(X, F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^1(\bar X, \bar F)^G \ar[d] \\ 0 \ar[r] & H^1(G, H^0(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)) \ar[r] & H^1(X_o, F_o) \ar[r] & H^1(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)^G } \] where the extreme vertical arrows are isomorphisms according to (5.2). Let \(\alpha\), \(\beta \in H^1(X, F)\) be classes which have the same image in \(H^1(X_o, F_o)\), and let \(F^\alpha\) be the beam obtained by twisting \(F\) using \(\alpha\) [1]. Let \(\beta'\) be the class \(H^1(X, F^\alpha)\) corresponding to \(\beta\). So, since the class \(\alpha'\) corresponding to \(\alpha\) is null, the image of \(\beta'\) in \(H^1(X_o, (F^\alpha)_o)\) is null.

Let's examine the diagram deduced from (5.5.1) by replacing \(F\) by \(F^\alpha\): we find that the image of \(\beta'\) in \(H^1(\bar X, \bar F^\alpha)^G\) is zero, therefore that \(\beta'\) is image of an element of \(H^1(G, H^0(\bar X, \bar F))\), and that this element must be null. So \(\beta'\) is null, i.e. \(\beta' = \alpha'\), hence \(\beta = \alpha\). This gives the injectivity of the morphism of (ii).

Now suppose that \(F\) is a bundle of ind-finite groups, and we prove the surjectivity of the arrow (ii). But it is enough to demonstrate that the functor \(F \mapsto H^1(X_o, F_o)\) is erasable. Indeed, let \(0 \to F \xrightarrow{u} G\) be a injection which erases this functor. Or \(C = G/F\). We have a sequence morphism accurate (4.4) \[ \xymatrix{ H^0(X, C) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^1(X, F) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{\varepsilon} & H^1(X, G) \ar[d] \\ H^0(X_o, C_o) \ar[r] & H^1(X_o, F_o) \ar[r]^{u^1_o} & H^1(X_o, G_o) } \] where the morphism \(u^1_o\) is zero, which implies the surjectivity of \(\varepsilon\).

Let us demonstrate that the functor \(F \mapsto H^1(X_o, F_o)\) is erasable for bundles \(F\) of indefinite groups. Let \(F \xrightarrow{u} C\) be the “Godement resolution” (3.4). We have \[ H^1(G, H^0(\bar X_o, \bar C_o)) \simeq H^1(G, H^0(\bar X, \bar C)) \subset H^1(X, C) = 0 \] In addition, we have the commutative square: \[ \xymatrix{ H^1(\bar X, \bar F) \ar[r]^{\sim} \ar[d] & H^1(\bar X_o, \bar F_o) \ar[d] \\ 0 = H^1(\bar X, \bar C) \ar[r] & H^1(\bar X_o, \bar C_o) } \] (since \(H^1(\bar X, \bar C) = \varinjlim H^1(X', C) = 0\), \(X'/X\) is equal), so is zero. We therefore have a morphism of exact sequences \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & H^1(G, H^0(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^1(X_o, F_o) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{\beta} & H^1(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)^G \ar[d]^{\alpha^G} \\ 0 \ar[r] & & H^1(X_o, C_o) \ar[r] & H^1(\bar X_o, \bar C_o)^G } \] where \(\alpha^G\) is null, which implies that \(\beta\) is also null, hence erasability.

Let us finally prove assertion (iii) of (5.5): We have a morphism of spectral sequences \[ \xymatrix{ E_2^{p,q} = H^p(G, H^q(\bar X, \bar F)) \ar@{=>}[r] \ar[d] & H^n(X, F) \ar[d]^{\varphi} \\ H^p(G, H^q(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)) \ar@{=>}[r] & H^n(X_o, F_o) } \] According to (5.2), the morphisms \(H^q(\bar X, \bar F) \longrightarrow H^q(\bar X_o, \bar F_o)\) are isomorphisms. This implies that \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism for \(E_2^{p,q}\), so for outcomes- ments, hence the result, cqfd.

5.6. Let us examine (5.5) (i) and (ii) in the case where \(F\) is constant: Let \(F = T_X\) a constant sheaf of sets on the schema \(X\), with value \(T\). So \(F(X)\) is none other than the set of locally constant applications of \(X\) in \(T\). If \(X_o\) is a subschema of \(X\), and if \(T\) has at least two elements, we conclude that \(F(X) \to F(X_o)\) is bijective if and only if the application \(U \mapsto U_o = U \cap X_o\) of the set \(\theta(X)\) of both open and closed parts of \(X\) in the en- seems analogous \(\theta(X_o)\) is bijective. If the set \(\pi_o(X)\) of components related to \(X\) is finished, it is the same as saying that the application \(\pi_o(X_o) \longrightarrow \pi_o(X)\) is bijective. Thus, (5.5) (i) can also be reformulated in the following way (taking into account that in this case \(X_o\) is of finite type on a body, therefore is Noetherian, therefore \(\pi_o(X_o)\) is finite):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a Henselian ring, \(s_o\) its closed point. Let \(f : X \to S\) be a proper morphism and \(X_o\) the closed fiber of \(X/S\). So the application \(\pi_o(X_o) \to \pi_o(X)\) induced by \(X_o \to X\) is bijective.
(In particular, this implies that the set \(\pi_o(X)\) is finite). We we will directly demonstrate (5.7) in the Noetherian case:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8

Statement (5.7) is true if \(S\) is Noetherian (*).

Demonstration. In the Noetherian case, \(\pi_o(X)\) is certainly finite, and by replacing \(X\) by a connected component we can assume that \(\pi_o(X)\) is a one-element set, i.e. \(X\) is connected and non-empty. It is necessary prove that then \(X_o\) is also connected and non-empty. But the image of \(X\) in \(S\) is closed since \(f\) is clean and non-empty because \(X\) is non-empty, therefore contains \(s_o\), which explains that \(X_o\) is non-empty. Either \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} \ar[r]^{f'} & S' \ar[dl]^{\pi} \\ S & } \] the Stein factorization of \(f\) (EGA III 4.3.3) so that \(S'/S\) is finite and \(S'\) is connected. We know that each fiber of \(f'\) is non-empty connected, and the space underlying \(X_o\) is the union of the spaces underlying the fibers closed from \(S'\). Everything therefore amounts to demonstrating that \(S'\) is the spectrum of a ring local. But since \(S\) is Henselian, the ring \(A'\) of \(S'\) is product of rings local (VII 4.1 (i)), so \(A'\) is local since \(S'\) is connected, which proves 5.8.

5.9.0. Now suppose that \(F = G_X\) is a constant bundle of groups, of value \(G\), where \(G\) is a finite group. Then \(H^1(X, G_X)\) classifies the coatings principal Galoisians of \(X\), of group \(G\) (VII 2.1). Corollary 5.5 (ii) says that the morphism \(X_o \to X\) induces a bijection between the sets of classes of these coverings. It therefore follows, from the definition of \(\pi_1\), that 5.5 (ii) for sheaves of constant finite groups (in the presence of (i)) equals:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.9

(Specialization theorem for the fundamental group). With the notations of (5.5), suppose \(X\) non-empty connected. Then \(X_o\) is connected non-empty, and if \(\bar x_o\) is a geometric point of \(X_o\), the morphism of groups fundamentals \[ \pi_1(X_o, \bar x_o) \longrightarrow \pi_1(X, \bar x_o) \] is bijective.
On the other hand, according to Galois theory (SGA V) statement 5.9 (in the related \(X_o\) case, which we can assume, thanks to 5.7) is equivalent to

blocks.label: 5.9 bis
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.9 bis

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a Henselian ring, \(s_o\) the point closed of \(S\), \(f : X \to S\) a proper morphism, and \(X_o\) the closed fiber of \(X/S\). For a diagram \(Z\), let us denote \(\operatorname{Et}(Z)\) the category of flat coverings of \(Z\). Then the restriction functor \[ \operatorname{Et}(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Et}(X_o) \] is an equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.10

Recall that 5.9 has already been demonstrated (SGA 1 case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a complete Noetherian local ring. Unfortunately we cannot use this result to prove 5.9 in general (*), and the demonstration given of 5.9, in the present presentation and the following, is very different from that given in SGA 1 X.

5.11. We can give another "non-commutative" variant of (5.5) (ii), in using the 2-cohomology theory of J. GIRAUD (cf. Thesis of J. GIRAUD, in preparation). Suppose \(S\) Noetherian. Let's take a link \(L\) on \(X\), and suppose that \(L\) is realized locally (for the sparse topology) by a beam in indefinite groups. Consider the application \[ \varphi^{(2)} : H^2(X, L) \longrightarrow H^2(X_o, L_o) , \] where \(L_o\) is the restriction of \(L\) to \(X_o\). So we have \[ (5.11.1) \qquad (\varphi^{(2)})^{-1}(H^2(X_o, L_o)') = H^2(X, L)' , \] where the "primes" designate the parts formed from neutral elements.
Taking into account 5.5 (iii), this statement is also equivalent to the following:

For any sheaf \(\mathscr{G}\) on \(X\), of link \(L\) as above, designating by \(\mathscr{G}_o\) the restriction of \(\mathscr{G}\) to \(X_o\), the restriction functor on the categories of sections \[ \mathscr{G}(X) \longrightarrow \mathscr{G}_o(X_o) \] is an equivalence of categories.

The fact that this functor is fully faithful results in fact from 5.5 (i), and the fact that it is essentially surjective when \(\mathscr{G} \neq \emptyset\) i.e. when the class \(\xi \in H^2(X, L)\) of \(\mathscr{G}\) is neutral, results from 5.5 (ii). He It therefore remains to be expressed that \(\mathscr{G}_o(X_o) \neq \emptyset\) implies \(\mathscr{G}(X) \neq \emptyset\), which is not other than (5.11.1).

We will not give here the proof of (5.11.1), and we will let us simply point out that for any Noetherian schema \(X\) and any sub- closed diagram, we can actually show that the validity of statements 5.5 (i) (ii) implies that of (5.11.1); the demonstration is done quite elementary, using the descent result VIII 9.4 a).

footnotes.html: (*) The situation has changed since these lines were written, cf. th. 3.1 in M. Artin, Algebraic approximation of structures over complete local rings (forthcoming).

blocks.html: 6. First reductions
We use the abbreviation \((f,F,g)\) to refer to data from 5.1 in any of the three cases (i), (ii) or (iii) of this theorem.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.1

For 5.1 to be true for data \((f,F,g)\), where \(f,F\) is set, and \(g\) any, it suffices that for all \(S\)-schema \(\bar S\), strict localization of a schema locally of finite type \(S'\) on \(S\) at a point which is closed in its fiber, the theorem is true for \((\bar f, \bar F, \bar g)\), where \(\bar f, \bar F\) are deduced from \(f, F\) by the change base \(\bar S \to S\), and where \(\bar g : \bar s \to \bar S\) is the inclusion of the closed point of \(\bar S\).

Demonstration. We can obviously assume \(S,S'\) affines, ring spectra \(A,A'\). Then \(A'\) is the inductive limit of its sub-\(A\)-algebras of finite type \(A'_i\), and using the theory of passage to the limit (VII 5.11 and 5.14), we is reduced to proving 5.1 for \((f, F, g_i)\) where \(g_i : \operatorname{Spec}(A'_i) = S'_i \to S\), this which brings us back to the case where \(g\) is of finite type. To prove that homomor- base change phism are isomorphisms, it is enough to prove that they induce isomorphisms on the fibers of the bundles involved, and just look at the geometric fibers at points \(s'\) of \(S'\) which are closed in their fiber on \(S\) (VIII 3.13). Let \(\bar s'\) be a geometric point of \(S'\) corresponding to a separable fence of \(k(s')\); as \(s'\) is algebraic on the corresponding point \(s\) of \(S\), \(k(\bar s')\) is therefore also a separable fence by \(k(s)\). Let us denote by \(\bar s\) the pre-schema \(\bar s'\), considered to be above \(S\), i.e. considered as geometric point of \(S\), and let us consider the localities strict \(\bar S\) and \(\bar S'\) from \(S\) to \(\bar s\) and \(\bar s'\) respectively, so that we have a square commutative. \[ \xymatrix{ \bar s' \ar[r] \ar[d] & \bar s \ar[d] \\ \bar S' \ar[r] & \bar S } \] where the first horizontal arrow is an isomorphism. Considering VIII 5.2 and 5.3, the homomorphism induced on the fibers in \(\bar s\) by the homomorphism of base change relative to \((f,F,g)\) identifies with homomorphism \[ H^i(\bar X, \bar F) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar X', \bar F') \] induced by \(\bar X' \to \bar X\), where \(\bar X\) and \(\bar X'\) denote the patterns deduced from \(X\) by the base changes \(\bar S \to S\) and \(\bar S' \to S\). But the commutative square above obviously provides a commutative square \[ \xymatrix{ H^i(\bar X_{\bar s}, \bar F) \ar[r] & H^i(\bar X'_{\bar s}, \bar F') \\ H^i(\bar X, \bar F) \ar[r] \ar[u] & H^i(\bar X', \bar F') \ar[u] } \] where the first horizontal arrow is still an isomorphism, so the second horizontal arrow is an isomorphism as soon as the two vertical arrows are. But this is what the hypothesis of (6.1) ensures, cqfd.

6.1.1 Thus, to prove 5.1 in a situation \((F,f,-)\), we are reduced to study the special case 5.5, with \(S\) strictly local. This leads us to study in a general way the situation where we have a morphism \(h : Y \to X\) of diagrams, and where we propose to give the general conditions subject to which the corresponding homomorphisms \[ H^i(X, F) \longrightarrow H^i(Y, h^*(F)) \] are bijective. This issue will give some auxiliary results easy on this situation, that (for the convenience of later references) we will state with more generality and precision than would be necessary sary for the demonstration of 5.1.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2

Let \(C,C'\) be two abelian categories, \(T^\bullet = (T^i)\) and \(T'^\bullet = (T'^i)\) two cohomological functors of \(C\) in \(C'\), \(\varphi = (\varphi^i)\) a homomorphism of cohomological functors from \(T^\bullet\) into \(T'^\bullet\). We assume \(T^\bullet\) and \(T'^\bullet\) zero in degrees \(< 0\), and \(T^i\) erasable for \(i > 0\). Let \(n\) be an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. a) \(\varphi^i\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\), a monomorphism for \(i = n + 1\).
  2. b) (If \(n \geq -1\)). The homomorphism \(\varphi^i\) is a monomorphism if \(i = 0\), and a epimorphism if \(i \leq n\).
  3. c) (If \(n \geq 0\)). The homomorphism \(\varphi^0\) is an isomorphism, and the \(T'^i\) are deleted. cables for \(0 < i \leq n\).

If these conditions are met, and if \(A \in \operatorname{Ob}(C)\), then \(\varphi^{n+1}(A) : T^{n+1}(A) \longrightarrow T'^{n+1}(A)\) is an epimorphism (hence an isomorphism) if and only if \(T'^{n+1}(A)\) is erasable, i.e. if there exists a monomorphism \(A \to B\) such that the corresponding homomorphism \(T'^{n+1}(A) \to T'^{n+1}(B)\) is zero. If \(C' = (\mathrm{Ab})\) (category of abelian groups), and if \(\xi \in T'^{n+1}(A)\), then \(\xi\) is in the image of \(T^{n+1}(A)\) if and only if \(\xi\) is erasable, i.e. if it exists a monomorphism \(A \to B\) such that the image of \(\xi\) in \(T'^{n+1}(B)\) is zero.

Demonstration. Obviously a) implies b) and c). As the assumptions made on \(T^\bullet\) imply that the \(T^i\) \((i > 0)\) are the right satellites of \(T^0\), the im- plication c) \(\Longrightarrow\) b) results from the axiomatic characterization of the functors satellites; it also results, by induction on \(n\), from the last assertion of 6.2, which is demonstrated by the well-known argument that we dispense repeat here (see demonstration of 6.″ below). The implication b) \(\Longrightarrow\) c) is trivial if \(n = 1\), and if \(n \geq 0\) we proceed by induction on \(n\): the hypo- recurrence thesis allows us to assume that \(\varphi^i\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n-1\), a monomorphism for \(i = n\), therefore by the hypothesis an isomorphism also for \(i = n\), and it remains to show that it is a monomorphism for \(i = n+1\), using that \(\varphi^n\) is an isomorphism. Let \(A \to B\) be a monomorphism which erases \(T^{n+1}\), hence an exact sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) in \(C\), and a homomorphism of exact sequences in \(C'\): \[ \xymatrix{ T^n(B) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^n(C) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{n+1}(A) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{n+1}(B) \ar[d] \\ T'^n(B) \ar[r] & T'^n(C) \ar[r] & T'^{n+1}(A) \ar[r] & T'^{n+1}(B) } \] where the first two vertical arrows are isomorphisms according to this that we have just seen, and \(T^{n+1}(A) \to T^{n+1}(B)\) is zero by construction. So the five lemma implies that \(T^{n+1}(A) \to T'^{n+1}(A)\) is a monomorphism, cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Suppose that \(T^\bullet, T'^\bullet\) comes from cohomological functors (denoted by the same symbols) on the right derived category [3] \(D^+(C)\), with value in \(C'\); \(T^i\) \((i > 0)\) are not necessarily assumed to be erasable. We assume that \(K^\bullet \in D^+(C)\), \(H^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i < 0\) implies \(T^i(K^\bullet) = T'^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i < 0\). Let \(K^\bullet \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(C)\) be a left-bounded complex in \(C\) such that \(H^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i < 0\).

  1. (i) If condition a) of 6.2 is satisfied, then the homomorphism \(\varphi^i(K^\bullet) = T^i(K^\bullet) \to T'^i(K^\bullet)\) is an isomorphism for \(i < n\), a monomorphism for \(i = n+1\).
  2. (ii) Let \(L^\bullet \in \operatorname{Ob} D^+(C)\) be further such that \(H^i(L^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i < 0\), and let \(u : L^\bullet \to K^\bullet\) a homomorphism in \(D^+(C)\), inducing an injected homomorphism tif \(H^0(u) : H^0(L^\bullet) \to H^0(K^\bullet)\). So, for \(T^{n+1}(L^\bullet) \to T'^{n+1}(L^\bullet)\) to be surjective, it is necessary and sufficient that the image of \(T'^{n+1}(L^\bullet)\) in \(T'^{n+1}(K^\bullet)\) be contained in that of \(T^{n+1}(K^\bullet)\). If \(C' = (\mathrm{Ab})\), and if \(\xi \in T'^{n+1}(L^\bullet)\), then \(\xi\) is in the image of \(T^{n+1}(L^\bullet)\) if and only if its image in \(T'^{n+1}(K^\bullet)\) is in that of \(T^{n+1}(K^\bullet)\).

Recall that the hypothesis that \(T^\bullet\) and \(T'^\bullet\) come from functors cohomologies on \(D^+(C)\) can be explained by saying that they come from cohomological functors on the abelian category of complexes bounded to left of \(C\), switching to translations of degrees, transforming homomor- phisms from homotopic complexes to zero into zero homomorphisms of \(C'\), and transforming any homomorphism \(u : K^\bullet \to L^\bullet\) of complexes which is a quasi- isomorphism (i.e. which induces isomorphisms \(H^i(K^\bullet) \to H^i(L^\bullet)\)) into one isomorphism of \(C'\). Practically, all cohomological functors of \(C\) in \(C'\) that we encounter are obtained like this.

Demonstration of 6.3. Let us first prove (i). The assertion is obvious if \(H^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i \leq n+1\), because then according to the imposed degree properties at \(T^\bullet\), \(T'^\bullet\), we will have \(T^i(K^\bullet) = T'^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i \leq n+1\). Or on the other hand \(m\) an integer \(\geq 0\) such as \(H^i(K^\bullet) = 0\) for \(i < m\): there exist, for example \(m = 0\) does the trick. We proceed by backward induction on \(m\), the assertion being proven if \(m = n+2\). We therefore assume the assertion proven for \(m' > m\), and prove it for \(m\). According to the hypothesis on the degrees of \(K^\bullet\), \(K^\bullet\) is quasi-isomorphic to a degree complex \(\geq m\), so we can assume to degrees \(\geq m\). It follows an exact sequence of complexes \[ 0 \longrightarrow H^m [-m] \longrightarrow K^\bullet \longrightarrow K'^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 , \] where \(H^m = H^m(K^\bullet)\), where the sign \([-m]\) indicates translation of \(-m\) on the degrees of a complex, and where \(K'^\bullet\) is defined as cokernel. So the cohomology objects of \(K'^\bullet\) are those of \(K^\bullet\) in degrees \(\geq m+1\), and zero in degrees \(\leq m+1\); in particular link, we can apply the recurrence hypothesis to \(K'^\bullet\). The exact sequel previous gives rise to a homomorphism of exact sequences \[ \xymatrix{ T^{i-1}(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{i-m}(H^m) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^i(K^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^i(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{i+1-m}(H^m) \ar[d] \\ T'^{i-1}(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^{i-m}(H^m) \ar[r] & T'^i(K^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^i(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^{i+1-m}(H^m) } \] Then doing \(i \leq n\) in the previous diagram, and taking into account the hypo- thesis of recurrence and \(m \geq 0\), we find that the vertical arrows around of the median vertical arrow are isomorphisms and a fortiori epi- morphisms, and the right vertical arrow is a monomorphism, so by the lemma of five the median vertical arrow is an epimorphism. Doing \(i \leq n+1\), we also find the vertical arrows around the median are monomorphisms, the left vertical arrow being an isomorphism and a fortiori an epimorphism, from which we conclude by the lemma of five that the middle vertical arrow is a monomorphism, which establishes 6.3 (i).

To establish the second assertion 6.3 (ii), we similarly use the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow L^\bullet \longrightarrow K^\bullet \longrightarrow K'^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 , \] where \(K^\bullet\) is defined as the mapping-cylinder of \(L^\bullet \to K^\bullet\), so is still positive degree cohomology. The usual “diagram hunt” in \[ \xymatrix{ T^n(K^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^n(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{n+1}(L^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{n+1}(K^\bullet) \ar[r] \ar[d] & T^{n+1}(K'^\bullet) \ar[d] \\ T'^n(K^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^n(K'^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^{n+1}(L^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^{n+1}(K^\bullet) \ar[r] & T'^{n+1}(K'^\bullet) } \] then gives the last assertion of 6.3, taking into account the fact that (by virtue of the first part already demonstrated) the first two vertical arrows are isomorphisms, the last two are monomorphisms. This completes the demonstration of 6.3.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4

Theorem (5.1) is true if \(f\) is finite.
This immediately follows from VIII 5.5 and 5.8.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5

Let \(h : Y \to X\) be a morphism of quasi-compact schemas and almost separated. In (i) below, \(I\) denotes a given set having at least colon, in (ii) and (iii), \(\mathbb{L}\) denotes a non-empty part of the set \(\mathbb{P}\) prime numbers, and in (iii) \(n\) denotes an integer.

(i) The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. a) For any bundle of sets \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical map \[ H^0(X,F) \longrightarrow H^0(Y,h^*(F)) \] is injective (resp. bijective).
  2. b) For any finite morphism \(X' \to X\), denoting by \(h' : Y' \to X'\) the morphism deduced from \(h\) by the change of base \(X' \to X\), the application canonical \[ H^0(X', I_{X'}) \longrightarrow H^0(Y', I_{Y'}) \] deduced from \(h'\) is injective (resp. bijective).
  3. b') With the notations of b), the application \(U \mapsto h'^{-1}(U)\) of the en- seems parts of \(Y'\) both open and closed throughout parts of \(X'\) both open and closed is injective (resp. bijective).
  4. b'') (If \(X\) locally Noetherian). With the notations of b), the ap- induced plication on sets of related components \[ \pi_0(h') : \pi_0(Y') \longrightarrow \pi_0(X') \] is surjective (resp. bijective), or again: if \(X'\) is non-empty (resp. connected non-empty) it is the same for \(Y'\).

Moreover, if these conditions are satisfied, then for any bundle in groups \(F\) on \(X\), the functor \(P \mapsto h^*(P)\) of the category of torsors (=homogeneous main fibers) under \(F\) in the category of torsors under \(h^*(F)\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful), and a fortiori, in the respective case, the canonical application \[ H^1(X,F) \longrightarrow H^1(Y,h^*(F)) \] is injective. Finally, under the same hypotheses, the functor \(X' \mapsto Y' = X' \times_X Y\) of the flat coatings category of \(X\) in the coatings category stalls of \(Y\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

(ii) The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. a) For any sheaf \(F\) of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-finite groups on \(X\), the application canonical \[ H^i(X,F) \longrightarrow H^i(Y,h^*(F)) \] is bijective for \(i = 0,1\).
  2. b) For any finite morphism \(X' \to X\), denoting by \(h' : Y' \to X'\) the morphism deduced from \(h\) by base change, and for any finite \(\mathbb{L}\)-group ordinary \(G\), the canonical application \[ H^i(X', G_{X'}) \longrightarrow H^i(Y', G_{Y'}) \] is bijective for \(i = 0,1\).
  3. b') (If \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P}\)). With the notations of b), the inverse image functor by \(h'\) induces an equivalence of the category of flat coatings of \(X'\) with the flat coatings category of \(Y'\).
  4. b'') (If \(X\) Noetherian, \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P}\)). With the notations of b), if \(X'\) is non-empty, it is the same for \(Y'\), and if \(y'\) is a geometric point of \(Y'\), \(x'\) its image in \(X'\), the canonical application \[ \pi_i(Y',y') \longrightarrow \pi_i(X',x') , \quad \text{pour } i = 0,1 , \] is bijective.

(iii) The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. a) For any sheaf \(F\) of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion on \(X\), the homomorphism \[ H^i(X,F) \longrightarrow H^i(Y,h^*(F)) \] is an isomorphism if \(i \leq n\), a monomorphism if \(i = n+1\).
  2. a') (If \(n \geq -1\)). For any \(F\) as above, the homomorphism envi- wise is injective if \(i = 0\), and surjective if \(i \leq n\).
  3. b) (If \(n \geq -1\)). For any finite morphism \(X' \to X\), denoting by \(h' : Y' \to X'\) the morphism deduced from \(h\) by base change \(X' \to X\), and for all \(\ell \in \mathbb{L}\), \(\nu > 0\), the canonical homomorphism \[ H^i(X', (\mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z})_{X'}) \longrightarrow H^i(Y', (\mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z})_{Y'}) \] is injective for \(i = 0\), surjective for \(i \leq n\).
In addition, we have the following complement to the previous statement:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.6

(i) Suppose the unresolved condition 6.5 (i) a) is satisfied, let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets on \(X\), and let \(\xi \in H^0(Y,h^*(F))\). Suppose that there exists a monomorphism of bundles of sets \(F \to G\) such that the image of \(\xi\) in \(H^0(Y,h^*(G))\) or in the image of \(H^0(X,G)\). So \(\xi\) is in the picture by \(H^0(X,F)\).

(ii) Suppose condition 6.5 (i) a) is satisfied, i.e. \(F\) a beam of groups on \(X\), and either \(\xi \in H^1(Y,h^*(F))\). Suppose that there exists a monomor- group bundle phism \(F \to G\) such as the image of \(\xi\) in \(H^1(Y,h^*(G))\) either in the image of \(H^1(X,G)\), then \(\xi\) is in the image of \(H^1(X,F)\).

(iii) Suppose condition 6.5 (iii) a) is satisfied (for a given value \(n\)), let \(F\) be a \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist beam on \(X\) and let \(\xi \in H^{n+1}(Y,h^*(F))\). Suppose that there exists a \(F \to G\) monomorphism of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion sheaves, such that the image of \(\xi\) in \(H^{n+1}(Y,h^*(G))\) or in the image of \(H^{n+1}(X,G)\), then \(\xi\) is in the image of \(H^{n+1}(X,F)\).

Demonstration of 6.5 and 6.6. Let us first prove 6.6. The case of 6.6 (iii) is a special case of the last assertion in 6.3, applied to the case where \(C\) is the category of abelian sheaves of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion on \(X\), \(C'\) is the category- gorie (Ab), and \(T^i(F) = H^i(X,F)\), \(T'^i(F) = H^i(Y,h^*(F))\) (note that \(T'^\bullet\) is indeed a cohomological functor in \(F\), thanks to the fact that the functor \(h^*\) is exact). Of course, here the consideration of complexes as in 6.3 is unnecessary (it will be convenient for us later (6.8)) and we only use the last assertion of 6.3.

To prove (i), consider the amalgamated sum \(H = G \amalg_F G\), limit inductive, in the category of bundles of sets on \(X\), of the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r] \ar[dr] & G \\ & G } \] The usual accuracy properties of the set sheaf topos show, as \(F \to G\) is a monomorphism, that we have an exact diagram of set bundles on \(X\) \[ F \longrightarrow G \rightrightarrows H \] (which can be expressed by saying that in a topos, any monomorphism is effective, and prove by reducing oneself as usual to the category of sets). As the functors \(H^0\) and \(h^*\) are left exact, we conclude a homomorphism of exact diagrams of sets \[ \xymatrix{ H^0(X,F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^0(X,G) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] \ar[d] & H^0(X,H) \ar[d] \\ H^0(Y,h^*(F)) \ar[r] & H^0(Y,h^*(G)) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & H^0(Y,h^*(H)) } \] where the vertical arrows are injective. Immediate “diagram hunting” then proves assertion 6.6 (i).

To prove 6.6 (ii), we will first prove the second assertion- tion of 6.5 (i), know that subject to condition a) of 6.5 (i), for all bundle of groups \(F\) on \(X\), the functor \(P \mapsto h^*(P)\), of the category of torsors under \(F\) in the category of torsors under \(h^*(F)\), is faithful (resp. fully faithful), therefore, in the respective case, induces an injection on the \(H^1\). For this, if \(P\) and \(P'\) are two torsors under \(F\), let us denote by \(\underline{\operatorname{Isom}}_F(P,P')\) the bundle of \(F\)-isomorphisms of \(P\) on \(P'\). It is immediate that the training of this beam switches to any extension of the base, in particular to the ex- base voltage by \(h\). (This is an assertion valid for any morphism of sites). Using the hypothesis on the effect of \(h^*\) on the \(H^0\) for the beam \(\underline{\operatorname{Isom}}_F(P,P')\), we find that the application \(\underline{\operatorname{Isom}}_F(P,P') \to \underline{\operatorname{Isom}}_F(h^*(P),h^*(P'))\) is injective (resp. bijective), which means that the functor \(P \mapsto h^*(P)\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful). (NB in the torso category under a bundle in groups, any homomorphism is an isomorphism). Let us note on the other hand, that if \(F\) is a group sub-sheaf of a group beam groups \(G\), then the data of a torso \(P\) under \(F\) returns to the data of a torso under \(G\) (namely that deduced from \(P\) by extension \(F \to G\) of the Group structural), equipped with a section of \(Q/F\) (which is indeed interpreted as a "Structural Group restriction" from \(G\) to \(F\)). This being said, with the notations of 6.6 (ii), as the image \(\eta\) of \(\xi\) in \(H^1(Y,h^*(G))\) comes from an element of \(H^1(X,G)\), the latter is defined by a torso \(Q\) under \(G\), so that \(\eta\) is the class of the torso \(h^*(Q)\) under \(h^*(G)\). The fact that \(\eta\) comes from a \(\xi \in H^1(Y,h^*(F))\) is then explained by saying that \(\xi\) is the class of \(h^*(F)\)-tor- ser defined by \(h^*(Q)\) and a suitable section of \(h^*(Q)/h^*(F)\). Like the functor \(h^*\) is exact on the right, this last bundle is none other than \(h^*(Q/F)\), and as \(h^*\) induces a bijection on the \(H^0\) of the set sheaves, it follows that the considered section of \(h^*(Q)/h^*(F)\) comes from a section of \(Q/F\). The latter then defines a \(F\)-torsor by restriction of the group structural, and the class of this torso in \(H^1(X,F)\) obviously has \(\xi\) as image in \(H^1(Y,h^*(F))\). This proves 6.6 (ii) and completes the proof of 6.6.

Let us now prove 6.5, starting with 6.5 (iii). Obviously a) implies a'), and the inverse implication is a special case of 6.2 (appli- qué again in the case where \(C\) is the category of the beams of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion on \(X\), and \(C'\) the category of sheaves of abelian groups), provided that we prove the erasure ability in \(C\) of functors \(H^i(X,F)\) for \(i > 0\). This does not in fact offer difficulty, but we can also dispense with proving this result, noting that \(F\) is the filtering inductive limit of its sub-beams \(F_i\) for which there exists an integer \(n > 0\) canceling \(F_i\) (IX 1.1). So \(h^*(F)\) is the inductive limit of \(h^*(F_i)\), and taking into account the switching of the cohomology in the inductive limit (VII 3.3), resulting from the hypotheses of quasi-compactness and quasi-separation made on \(X\) and \(Y\) (which did not have used for 6.6), we reduce ourselves to proving condition a) for the \(F_i\), this which brings us back to the case where there exists an integer \(m > 0\) such that \(mF = 0\). We can obviously suppose that the prime divisors of \(m\) are \(\in \mathbb{L}\), and we can then take for \(C\) the category of bundles of \(\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}\)-Modules on \(X\). Gold we know that in this category, the functors \(H^i(X, - )\) for \(i \geq 1\) are erasable ( ). This shows the equivalence of conditions a) and a') of 6.5(iii). On the other hand, applying a) to the direct image of a beam of \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist \(F'\) on \(X'\), \(X'\) ends on \(X\), which is \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist thanks to IX 1.2 (v), 1.6 (iii), and using 6.4 and VIII 5.5, we find that the homomorphism \[ H^i(X',F') \longrightarrow H^i(Y',h'^*(F')) \] is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\), a monomorphism for \(i = n+1\), hence a fortiori condition b). It remains to prove that this condition implies a'). For this, let us use the fact (IX 2.7.2) that any sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion \(F\) on \(X\) is limit inductive filtering of constructible \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist beams; then the argument of passage to the limit already used shows that in a'), we can limit ourselves in case \(F\) is buildable. By virtue of IX 2.14 there is then a number finite of finite morphisms \(p_i : X'_i \to X\), of constant sheaves of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion \(G_{X'_i}\) on \(X'_i\), and an injective homomorphism \[ F \longrightarrow \prod_i p_{i*}(G_{X'_i}) \] That said, if \(n = -1\), i.e. when the desired conclusion is reduced to the assertion of injectivity for \(H^0(X,F) \to H^0(Y,h^*(F))\), we immediately see that we can in this question replace \(F\) with a sheaf \(G\) such that there exists a monomorphism \(F \to G\), and consequently we can replace \(F\) by the product of \(p_{i*}(G_{X'_i})\). Taking into account 6.4, we are therefore reduced to proving the assertion by replacing \(h : Y \to X\) by \(h' : Y' \to X'\), and \(F\) by a constant beam on \(X'\) of value one finite group of \(\mathbb{L}\)-ordinary twist \(G\). But \(G\) is isomorphic to a sum of groups of the form \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z}\), with \(\ell \in \mathbb{L}\). We are therefore much reduced to check b). When \(n \geq 0\), we proceed in a similar way. By recurrence on \(n\), we can assume the desired conclusion (and consequently a)) proven for the integer \(n-1\) instead of \(n\). Using then 6.6 (iii), we see that we can again replace \(F\) by a sheaf \(G\) such that a monomorphism exists \(F \to G\), and we will again take for \(G\) the beam \(p_*(G_{X'})\). Considering 6.4 and the nullity of \(R^i p_*\) \((i \geq 1)\) for a finite morphism (VIII 5.5), this gives us brings back again to prove the surjectivity for \(H^n\) by replacing \(h : Y \to X\) by a \(h' : Y' \to X'\), and \(F\) by a constant beam on \(X'\) of value one finite group of \(\mathbb{L}\)-ordinary torsion. As the injectivity is already acquired thanks to the recurrence hypothesis and the implication a') \(\Longrightarrow\) a), we are still brought back, by unscrewing to \(G\), in the case where \(G\) is of the form \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z}\), that is to say in the case envisaged in condition b) of 6.5 (iii). This proves the case (iii) from 6.5.

Cases (i) and (ii) are demonstrated in exactly the same way, for which is the equivalence of conditions a) and b) in 6.5 (i) resp. 6.5 (ii): we use IX 2.7.2 and the switching of \(H^0\) and \(H^1\) to the inductive limits filtering beams (VII 5.14) to reduce to the case where \(F\) is cons- tructible, then IX 2.14, 6.6 (i)(ii) and VIII 5.8 to return to the specific case- considered in b). Furthermore, in (i), the equivalence of the conditions b), b') and b'') is trivial and is only given for memory, as is the fact that they imply the fidelity (resp. full fidelity) of the functor \(X' \mapsto Y'\) in the category of flat coatings of \(X\) in the category of smear coatings of \(Y\). It is the same for the equivalence of the conditions b), b') and b'') in 6.5 (ii), which were added for weight. The demonstration of 6.5 is thus completed.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 6.7

(*) 1) Obviously, the arguments proving 6.5 and 6.6 are very general and essentially trivial nature and would benefit from being cleared in abstract lemmas of the same water as 6.2 (the sheaves \(p_*(G_{X'})\) acting as cogenerators of the \(C\) category in which we work). We leave this pleasant exercise to the reader, and we limit ourselves to pointing out that essentially the same statement could be given by looking at functors such as \(R^i f_*\) instead of \(H^i\) functors. The same remarks apply to 6.8, 6.11 below.

2) When \(X\) is Noetherian, then the given arguments show as in the statement of conditions b) and their variants in 6.5 (i) (ii) (iii), we can limit ourselves to taking \(X'\) integrity; when \(X\) is universally Japanese, we can even take them as intact and normal; it is also possible without the Japanese restriction on \(X\), provided that morphisms are taken \(p : X' \to X\) integers instead of finite morphisms. We will not use of none of these variants in the rest of the seminar.

3) The proof of 6.6 does not use the quasi-comparison hypothesis cited and quasi-separation made on \(X\), and it is the same for the fact that in (i) (ii) (iii) condition a) implies the others, as well as for the equivalence of conditions b), b'), b'') between them. Let us note on the other hand that form b) of conditions 6.5 (i) shows that, for \(\mathbb{L} = \emptyset\), these are special cases (when \(X\) is quasi-separated quasi-compact) of 6.5 (iii), for \(n = -1\) resp. for \(n = 0\). This remains true (limiting ourselves to the conditions in the form a)) without the restriction stated on \(X\), as we see easily thanks to the fact that for any bundle of sets \(F\), and any number first \(\ell\), we can find two abelian sheaves of \(\ell\)-torsion \(G\) and \(H\), and a homomorphism \(u : G \to H\) of the underlying set sheaves, such that \(F\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of the zero section (we will take for \(G\) the \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z}\)-Free module generated by \(F\), and for \(H\) the constant beam \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z}\) on \(X\)). The same argument shows that (without restriction of quasi-compactness and quasi-separation) 6.5 (ii) a) (where it is even sufficient to do \(i = 0\)) implies the condition resp. 6.5(i) a); this subsequently allows, given that this The latter already implies the injectivity of \(H^1(X,F) \to H^1(Y,h^*(F))\) for all beam in groups \(F\) on \(X\), to limit itself in the statement of 6.5 (ii) a) to require for \(i = 1\) the surjectivity of \(H^1(X,F) \to H^1(Y,h^*(F))\). Unfortunately, the case 6.5 (ii) cannot be considered as a special case of 6.5 (iii), this which often obliges us to repeat in the non-commutative case an argument already essentially done in the commutative case. Note also that, except 6.10, the following results are stated so that they are valid without assumption of quasi-compactness and quasi-separation.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.8

(Descent lemma). With overall ratings of 6.5, supposewe also give a morphism \(p : \bar X \to X\), denote by \(\bar h : \bar Y \to \bar X\) the morphism deduced from \(h\) by the change of base \(p\), and by \(p_Y : \bar Y \to Y\) the canonical morphism. We assume either that \(p\) is surjective, or that \(h\) is a closed immersion and that \(X = p(\bar X) \cup h(Y)\).

(i) Suppose that the morphism \(\bar h : \bar Y \to \bar X\) satisfies the condition of in- jectivity (resp. bijectivity) of 6.5 (i) a). In the respective case, suppose more than the base change morphism \[ (*) \qquad h^*(p_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F)) \] is injective for any sheaf of sets \(\bar F\) over \(\bar X\). So the morphism \(h : Y \to X\) also satisfies the injectivity condition (resp. of bijec- tivity) of 6.5 (i) a).

(ii) Suppose that \(\bar h : \bar Y \to \bar X\) satisfies the condition of 6.5 (ii) a), and that for any bundle of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\), the change morphism base (*) is bijective. Then \(h : Y \to X\) also satisfies the condition of 6.5 (ii) a).

(iii) Suppose that \(\bar h : \bar Y \to \bar X\) satisfies the condition of 6.5 (iii) a), and that for any abelian sheaf \(\bar F\) of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion on \(\bar X\), the homomorphism of change of basis \[ h^*(R^i p_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow R^i p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F)) \] either bijective for \(i \leq n-1\), injective for \(i = n\) (where \(n\) is a given integer \(\geq 1\). Then the morphism \(h : Y \to X\) also satisfies the condition of 6.5 (iii) a).

Demonstration of 6.8. In the case where we do not assume \(p\) surjective, but \(h\) a closed immersion and \(X = p(\bar X) \cup h(Y)\), we consider the sum scheme \(\bar X'\) from \(\bar X\) and \(Y\), and the morphism \(p' : \bar X' \to X\) deduced from \(p\) and \(h\). Then \(p'\) is surjective, moreover we verify trivially, in each of the three cases en- faced (i) (ii) (iii), that the hypotheses made on the couple \((h,p)\) are still satisfied for the couple \((h,p')\). So this brings us back to the case where \(p\) is surjective, in which we will place ourselves in the following. In this case for any sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical homomorphism \[ F \longrightarrow p_*(p^*(F)) \] is injective. In case (i), to check the injectivity of \(H^0(X,F) \to H^0(Y,h^*(F))\), we are brought back immediately in case we replace \(F\) by \(p_*(\bar F)\) (where we put \(\bar F = p^*(F)\)). Now consider the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ H^0(X, p_*(\bar F)) \ar[r] \ar[dd]_{\wr} & H^0(Y, h^*(p_*(\bar F))) \ar[d] \\ & H^0(Y, p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) \ar[d]^{\wr} \\ H^0(\bar X, \bar F) \ar[r] & H^0(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F)) , } \] where the left vertical arrow and the second right vertical arrow are canonical isomorphisms, the first right vertical arrow coming from of the base change homomorphism by \(h\). By hypothesis the second horizontal arrow is injective, which immediately implies that it is even of the first, which proves the unfulfilled assertion of (i). To assure him- respée, we assume that the second horizontal arrow is surjective, moreover the base change homomorphism being a monomorphism by hypothesis, it is the same for the first right vertical arrow, therefore of the composed of the two right vertical arrows. It immediately follows that the first horizontal arrow is also surjective, which completes the proof (i). To prove (ii), thanks to the above and 6.5 (i) we are reduced to prove surjectivity for \(H^1(X,F) \to H^1(Y,h^*(F))\). Using 6.6 (ii) we are again brought back to prove it for \(F\) of the form \(p_*(\bar F)\), where \(\bar F\) is a bundle of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups on \(\bar X\) (IX 1.6 (ii)). Writing the exact sequences for the \(H^1\), (variants of (3.2)) for the morphisms \(p\) and \(p_Y\), we find a homomorphism of non-commutative exact sequences: \[ \xymatrix{ H^1(X, p_*(\bar F)) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^1(\bar X, \bar F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^0(X, R^1 p_*(\bar F)) \ar[d] \\ H^1(Y, p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) \ar[r] & H^1(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F)) \ar[r] & H^0(Y, R^1 p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) , } \] where in each line the first arrow is injective and identifies the first term to the reverse image, by the second arrow, of the point marked with the third term. By assumption on \(\bar h\), the median vertical arrow is bijective. Else On the other hand, the two extreme vertical arrows are factored respectively by \(H^1(Y,h^*(p_*(\bar F)))\) and \(H^0(Y,h^*(R^1 p_*(\bar F)))\), by composing the homomorphisms \(H^i(X,-) \to H^i(Y,-)\) (for \(i = 1\) and \(i = 0\) respectively) and homomorphisms deduced by applying \(H^i(Y,-)\) (for these same values of \(i\)) to the homomorphism base change \(h^*(p_*(\bar F)) \to p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) resp. \(h^*(R^1 p_*(\bar F)) \to R^1 p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\). As the first of these arrows is an isomorphism by hypothesis, it follows that the first vertical arrow in the diagram above identifies with the homo- morphism \(H^1(X,-) \to H^1(Y,-)\) that we want to study. To prove his bijec- tivity, it therefore remains to prove that the last vertical arrow of the diagram is injective. But according to what we have just said, it is made up of two applications, the first of which is a canonical homomorphism \(H^0(X,-) \to H^0(Y,-)\), therefore bijective, and the second is injective, because deduced by the exact left functor \(H^0(Y,-)\) of a homomorphism \(h^*(R^1 p_*(\bar F)) \to R^1 p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) which is a monomorphism. The latter does in fact easily results from the hypothesis of bijectivity made for the homo- base change morphism in previous dimension \(0\), for example using the second assertion of 6.5 (i) and the usual calculation of fibers of the upper direct images (VIII 5.3). This proves 6.8 (ii).

It remains to prove 6.8 (iii). For this, it will be more convenient for us, instead of directly using the \(F \to p_*(p^*(F))\) injection as above, to use homomorphism \[ F \longrightarrow Rp_*(\bar F) \] in the right derived category \(D^+(C)\), where \(C\) denotes the category of beams abelians on \(X\). Recall that by definition, \(Rp_*(\bar F)\) is the complex \(p_*(C(\bar F))\), where \(C(\bar F)\) is a complex injective resolution of \(\bar F\) in the category of abelian bundles on \(\bar X\). If \(K^\bullet\) is a complex over \(X\), we denote by \(H^i(X,K^\bullet)\) the hypercohomology groups of \(X\) with coefficients in \(K^\bullet\), and we uses analogous notations on \(Y\). By virtue of 6.3, we are brought back to prove surjectivity for \[ (1) \qquad H^n(X, Rp_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow H^n(Y, h^* Rp_*(\bar F)) \] But by hypothesis, the base change homomorphism \[ (2) \qquad h^*(Rp_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow Rp_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F)) \] induces an isomorphism on the cohomology sheaves in degrees \(\leq n-1\), and a monomorphism in degrees \(n\), which can be expressed by saying that the "mapping-cylinder" \(K^\bullet\) from the previous homomorphism has bundles of non-zero cohomology only in degrees \(\geq n\). So \(H^i(Y,K^\bullet) = 0\) if \(i < n\), which implies, by the exact cohomology sequence, that the homomorphisms \[ (3) \qquad H^i(Y, h^*(Rp_*(\bar F))) \longrightarrow H^i(Y, Rp_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) \] induced by the homomorphism above, are isomorphisms for \(i \leq n-1\), a monomorphism for \(i = n\). Consider the compound of (1) and (3) \[ (4) \qquad H^n(X, Rp_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow H^n(Y, h^*(Rp_*(\bar F))) \longrightarrow H^n(Y, Rp_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) . \] The extreme terms are respectively isomorphic, by virtue of the definitions, to \(H^n(\bar X, \bar F)\) and \(H^n(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F))\), and the compound of (4) is none other than the homomorphism deduced from \(\bar h\), which is bijective thanks to the hypothesis made on \(\bar h\). Like the second arrow of (4) is injective according to what we have just seen, the first is also bijective, which completes the proof of 6.8.
Moreover, we immediately see that the previous demonstration provides the following result, slightly more precise and more general:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.9

Ratings are 6.8. We assume more satisfaction made for \(h\) the condition not respected in 6.5 (i) (resp. the condition respected of 6.5 (i), resp. the condition of 6.5 (iii), with \(n \geq -1\)), and that for all bundle of sets \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\), the base change homomorphism \(h^*(p_*(\bar F)) \to p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) is injective (resp. bijective, resp. that for all beam of \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\), base change homomorphism \(h^* R^i p_*(\bar F) \to R^i p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) is bijective for \(i \leq n\), injective for \(i = n+1\)). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. a sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) on \(X\), and let \(\xi \in H^{n+1}(Y,h^*(F))\) (where we take \(n = -1\) in the case (i), \(n = 0\) in case (ii)). Let's set \(\bar F = p^*(F)\). So, for \(\xi\) to be in the image of \(H^{n+1}(X,F)\), it is necessary and sufficient that its inverse image in \(H^{n+1}(\bar Y, p_Y^*(h^*(F))) = H^{n+1}(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F))\) is in the image of \(H^{n+1}(\bar X, \bar F)\).
We will use this specified form of 6.8 to prove the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.10

Ratings are 6.5. We assume, for simplicity, verified the condition not met in 6.5 (i).

(i) So that the condition resp. of 6.5 (i) is satisfied (resp. for that we have 6.5 (ii) a), resp. for us to have 6.5 (iii) a)), we must and it it is enough that the following condition is satisfied: For any beam \(F\) of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. of abelian groups of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) on \(X\), all \(\xi \in H^0(Y,h^*(F))\) (resp. all \(\xi \in H^i(Y,h^*(F))\) with \(i = 0,1\), resp. any \(\xi \in H^i(Y,h^*(F))\) with \(i \leq n\)), and any non-empty closed part \(X'\) of \(X\), denoting by \(Y'\) its inverse image in \(Y\), there exists a morphism \(p : \bar X \to X\) satisfying the following conditions:

  1. 1°) Image \(p(\bar X)\) is contained in \(X'\) and contains a non-empty open of \(X'\).
  2. 2°) For any sheaf of sets \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\) (resp....) the homomorphism of base change \(h^*(p_*(\bar F)) \to p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) is an isomorphism (resp. for any bundle of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups \(\bar F\) over \(X\), base change homomorphism \(h^*(R^i p_*(\bar F)) \to R^i p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) for \(i = 0,1\) is bijective, resp. for everything abelian sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\), the base change homomorphism is bijective for \(i \leq n\)).
  3. 3°) The inverse image of \(\xi\) in \(H^i(\bar Y, p_Y^*(h^*(F))) = H^i(\bar Y, \bar h^*(p^*(F)))\) is contained in the image of \(H^i(\bar X, p^*(F))\).

(ii) Let \(n \geq -1\), and suppose that for any sheaf of sets \(F\) (resp. any bundle of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. any abelian beam of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) \(F\) on \(X\), the homomorphism \(H^i(X,F) \to H^i(Y,h^*(F))\) is bijective for \(i \leq n\), injective for \(i = n+1\), (in the first case, we suppose \(n = -1\), in the second, \(n = -1\) or \(0\)). Or \(\xi \in H^{n+1}(Y,h^*(F))\). So that \(\xi\) is in the image of \(H^{n+1}(X,F)\), it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfies the stated condition in (i) above, with \(i = n+1\).
Demonstration of 6.10. Using 6.5 and immediate recurrence on \(n\), we see that 6.10 (i) is a consequence of 6.10 (ii), that we will now holding demonstrate.

Suppose \(\xi\) is not in the image of \(H^n(X,F)\). Let \(\Phi\) be the seems closed parts \(X'\) of \(X\) such as image \(\xi\) in \(H^n(Y', h^*(F)|Y')\) is not contained in that of \(H^n(X', F|X')\). By hypothesis, \(X \in \Phi\), therefore \(\Phi\) is not empty. Let us order \(\Phi\) by the relation \(\supset\), and show that \(\Phi\) is inductive. For this, it suffices to prove that if \((X'_\lambda)\) is a totally ordered family- born from elements of \(\Phi\), and if \(X'\) is their intersection, then \(X' \in \Phi\). Now, equip- ing the \(X'_\lambda\) and \(X'\) of the reduced induced structure, we see that the morphisms \(X'_\lambda \to X\) are affine (since they are closed immersions), so the projective system of \(X'_\lambda\) satisfies the conditions envisaged in VII 5. Furthermore, we immediately see that \(X'\) is the projective limit of \(X'_\lambda\). Likewise, \(Y'\) is the projective limit of the projective system of \(Y'_\lambda\), which are affine on \(Y\). Using VII 5.8, we therefore find \[ \varinjlim H^n(X'_\lambda, F|X'_\lambda) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^n(X', F|X') , \quad \varinjlim H^n(Y'_\lambda, h^*(F)|Y'_\lambda) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^n(Y', h^*(F)|Y') . \] From this, we immediately conclude that if we had \((\xi|Y') \in \operatorname{Im} H^n(X', F|X')\), then there would exist an index \(\lambda\) such as \((\xi|Y'_\lambda) \in \operatorname{Im} H^n(X'_\lambda, F|X'_\lambda)\), which is absurd; so we have \(X' \in \Phi\). Therefore \(\Phi\) is inductive, and therefore contains an element minimal, i.e. \(X'\). As obviously \(\emptyset \notin \Phi\), \(X'\) is non-empty. Let's apply to \(\xi\) and to \(X'\) the hypothesis of 6.10, hence a morphism \(p : \bar X \to X\) satisfying the conditions 1°) to 3°) set out in 6.10. Note that we can even assume \(p\) surjective. Indeed, let \(U\) be a non-empty open of \(X'\) contained in \(p(\bar X)\), and let \(X'_1\) be its complement in \(X'\), so that by construction we have \(X'_1 \notin \Phi\). Using this relation, we immediately see that, setting \(\bar X_1 = \bar X \amalg X'_1\) and denoting by \(p_1 : \bar X_1 \to X\) the morphism defined by \(p\) and the inclusion of \(X'_1\) in \(X\), the morphism \(p_1\) still satisfies the same hypotheses as \(p\); moreover, it is surjective. We therefore assume \(p\) surjective, and now apply 6.9, replacing the morphism \(h : Y \to X\) with the morphism \(h' : Y' \to X'\) (\(X'\) being provided, say, with the reduced induced structure), and \(\xi\) by \(\xi|Y'\). We then concludes from 6.9 that we have \((\xi|Y') \in \operatorname{Im} H^n(X', F|X')\), which contradicts the relation \(X' \in \Phi\) and completes the proof of 6.10.

We will use 6.10 to bring back 5.5 in case \(f\) is a morphism projective; but for this, in the non-Noetherian case, we will need of a non-Noetherian variant of Chow's lemma, which will be given in number next.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.11

(Transitivity lemma). The ratings are those of 6.8, but we make no assumption of surjectivity relative to \(p\). We suppose that \(h\) satisfies condition (i) a) (resp. (ii) a), resp. (iii) a) of 6.5. We further assume, in case (i), that for any sheaf of sets \(\bar F\) on \(\bar X\), the base change homomorphism of 6.8 is a monomorphism (resp. an isomorphism); in case (ii), that \(\bar X\) is Noetherian and that for all bundle \(\bar F\) of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, base change homomorphism \(h^*(R^i p_*(\bar F)) \to R^i p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) is bijective for \(i = 0,1\); finally, in the case (iii), that the base change homomorphism is bijective for \(i \leq n\), injective for \(i = n+1\). Then the morphism \(\bar h : \bar Y \to \bar X\) also satisfies under condition (i) (resp. (ii), resp. (iii)) of 6.5.

Demonstration of 6.11. In case (i), we consider the commutative diagram of the proof of 6.8 (i). Here the assumptions imply that the first horizontal arrow and the first vertical arrow on the right are injective (resp. bijectives), so it is the same for the second horizontal arrow, what we wanted to establish. In case (ii), we give ourselves a sheaf \(\bar F\) of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups on \(\bar X\), and it must be shown that any element of \(H^1(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F))\) comes from an element of \(H^1(\bar X, \bar F)\). Under 6.6 (ii) applied to \(\bar h\), it suffices to find a monomorphism \(\bar F \to \bar G\) of sheaves in groups on \(\bar X\) which deletes \(H^1(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F))\). Now consider the second line of the diagram used in the proof of 6.8 (ii). It is sufficient to successively find a monomorphism \(\bar F \to \bar G\), with \(\bar G\) a sheaf in ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-finite groups, which erases the last term \(H^0(Y, R^1 p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F)))\), then a monomorphism of sheaves in groups \(\bar G \to \bar H\) which clears \(H^1(Y, p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F)))\). However, as we noticed in the demonstration of 6.8 (i), the two terms that need to be erased are isomor- phes respectively, by the extreme vertical arrows of the envisaged diagram, to \(H^0(X, R^1 p_*(\bar F))\) and to \(H^1(X, p_*(\bar F))\), taking into account the hypothesis on homomor- base change phisms made here on \(p,h\). Moreover, isomorphisms considered are obviously functional in \(\bar F\), so that it suffices to delete the two previous terms. But under 3.3 we can delete \(R^1 p_*(\bar F)\) (and fortiori \(H^0(X, R^1 p_*(\bar F))\)) by a monomorphism of \(\bar F\) in an ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-group \(\bar G\). It remains to erase \(H^1(X, p_*(\bar F))\), which is possible thanks to the fact that by the diagram envisaged, this last term is sent in \(H^1(\bar X, \bar F)\) by a mono- functorial morphism to \(\bar F\), and that \(H^1(\bar X, \bar F)\) is still erasable thanks to 3.3.

It remains to treat case (iii), and for this one again we start again- let's reverse the demonstration of 6.8 (*). It is necessary to prove that the homomorphism \[ H^i(\bar X, \bar F) \longrightarrow H^i(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F)) \] is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\), and a monomorphism for \(i = n+1\). But the previous homomorphisms are induced by a homomorphism of complexes \[ \mathbb{R}\Gamma_{\bar X}(\bar F) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}\Gamma_{\bar Y}(\bar h^*(\bar F)) , \] deduced from an injective resolution \(C(\bar F)\) and a homomorphism of \(\bar h^*(C(\bar F))\) (which is a resolution of \(\bar h^*(\bar F)\)) into an injective resolution of \(\bar h^*(\bar F)\). The above homomorphism also identifies with the compound homomorphism \[ \mathbb{R}\Gamma_X(Rp_*(\bar F)) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}\Gamma_Y(h^*(Rp_*(\bar F))) \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}\Gamma_Y(Rp_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))) , \] where the first homomorphism is the homomorphism \(h^*\) relative to \(Rp_*(\bar F)\), and the second is deduced from base change homomorphisms \(h^*(Rp_*(\bar F)) \to Rp_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) by applying \(\mathbb{R}\Gamma_Y\). The first homomorphism therefore induces an isomorphism on the cohomology groups of the complexes considered, under the assumption made on \(h\) and 6.3 (i). It is the same for the second, by virtue of the hypothesis that we made on base change homomorphisms, using the argument given in the proof of 6.8 (iii). Consequently, the same conclusion applies to composed of both, which completes proof 6.11.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 6.12

The argument given for 6.11 in cases (i) and (iii) gives yet another more precise result: to be able to conclude, for a beam \(\bar F\) given on \(\bar X\), that \(H^i(\bar X, \bar F) \to H^i(\bar Y, \bar h^*(\bar F))\) is bijective for \(i \leq n\), injective for \(i = n+1\), just assume

1°) the base change homomorphism \(h^*(R^i p_*(\bar F)) \to R^i p_{Y*}(\bar h^*(\bar F))\) is an isomorphism for \(i \leq n\), a monomorphism for \(i = n+1\), and

2°) for any pair of integers \(i,j \geq 0\), the homomorphism \(H^i(X, R^j p_*(\bar F)) \to H^i(Y, h^*(R^j p_*(\bar F)))\) induced by \(h\) is an isomorphism if \(i+j \leq n\), a monomorphism if \(i+j = n+1\).

It is plausible that we have an analogous result in case (ii), but its statement should necessarily involve 2-cohomology non-commutative. It is to avoid recourse to this theory that we have used a different argument in 6.10 (ii), using the erasing properties ability of the non-commutative \(H^1\), an argument which does not, however, give any results. tat, as above, for a fixed \(\bar F\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 6.13

The most important case where the conditions of 6.5 (i) (ii) (iii) are fulfilled is that envisaged in 5.5, which will be proven in the presentation following using the reductions made in this presentation. Another interesting case (where \(h\) is not a closed immersion like in 5.5 will be studied in XV. Let us also point out the case, quite close to 5.5, where \(X\) is the spectrum of a Noetherian ring, separate and complete for the defined topology by an ideal \(J\), and where \(Y = \operatorname{Spec}(A/J)\) is the closed subschema of \(X\) defined by \(J\). We then directly verify the conditions of 6.5 (ii) (with \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P}\)) under the form b'') (EGA IV 18.3.2); it is very plausible that the conditions of 6.5 (iii) are also verified for any \(n\), i.e. for any bundle of twist \(F\) on \(X\), the homomorphisms \(H^n(X,F) \to H^n(Y,F|Y)\) are bijective. We can make considerably more general conjectures, linked to a generalization to non-local schemes of the henselization construction. First of all, note that we do not know of an example, \(h\) being an immersion closed, where the conditions of 6.5 (ii) (iii) are satisfied (with \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P}\)) when the condition of 6.5 (i) is met, that is to say when the couple \((X,Y)\) is a Henselian couple in the terminology of (EGA IV 18.5.5); we can therefore conjecture that such a couple satisfies the conditions of 6.5 (ii) (iii) (with \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P}\), all \(n\)). On the other hand, we can ask ourselves under what conditions the following property for a pair \((X,Y)\) of a schema \(X\) quasi- complete and quasi-separated and a closed subschema \(Y\) implies that the couple is Henselian:

(*) For any schema \(X'\) spreads over \(X\), setting \(Y' = X' \times_X Y\), the application canonical cation \(\Gamma(Y'/Y) \to \Gamma(X'/X)\) is bijective.

(Cf. EGA IV 18.5.4 b)). This condition is obviously weaker a priori that the Henselian condition 6.5 (i); it is even strictly weaker, because it is verified for example if \(X\) is an irrespective normal projective schema ducible dimension \(\geq 2\) on a body \(k\), and \(Y\) a hyperplane section of \(X\) (as it easily follows from SGA 2 XII 2.1, where we do \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\)), but obviously the couple \((X,Y)\) is not Henselian. It is possible, however, that if \(X\) is affine, the condition (*) implies that \((X,Y)\) is a Henselian couple; if it were so, for any affine scheme \(X\) and any closed part \(Y\) of \(X\), a process of localization spread along \(Y\), analogous to that of the hen- selization of local rings, should allow a couple to be associated with it henselian \((X',Y)\), where \(X'\) is "proetal" on \(X\). Another hypothesis about \((X,Y)\) (suggested by results of Hironaka and Rossi on the contractibility of certain submanifolds of algebraic varieties), which would perhaps allow to deduce the Henselian property from the weaker property (*), is the following: the immersion \(Y \to X\) is regular of codimension 1, and the beam conormal \(\underline{N}_{Y/X} = \underline{J}/\underline{J}^2\) (where \(\underline{J}\) is the ideal that defines \(Y\) in \(X\)) is a sheaf invertible ample on \(Y\). (NB. In the case of the counter-example considered above, this conormal beam was an anti-ample opposite, i.e. its inverse being ample).

footnotes.html: (*) The editor recommends omitting the reading of these remarks, as well as (6.13), introduced surreptitiously (at the same time as various other more or less successful modifications of the original text) by an irreverent collaborator.
(*) We could also, instead of 6.3, use the Leray spectral sequence for \(p\), \(p_Y\).

blocks.html: 7. A variant of Chow's Lemma (*)
We will need the following variant of (EGA II 5.6.1) disbar- free of all Noetherian hypotheses or irreducibility.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1

Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme, and \(f : X \to S\) a separate morphism of finite type, with non-empty \(X\).
