There is a diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} & \bar X \ar[l]_{\pi} \ar[dl]^{\bar f} \\ S & } \] with \(\pi\) projective and \(\bar f\) quasi-projective, and a non-empty open \(U\) of \(X\), such that \(\pi\) induces an isomorphism of \(\bar U = U \times_X \bar X = \pi^{-1}(U)\) on \(U\).

Demonstration. Let us first note: Let \(i : X' \to X\) be a closed subschema of \(X/S\) and \(V \subset X'\) an open subschema in \(X\) and dense in \(X'\). If the lemma is true for \(X'\), it is true for \(X\). Indeed, either \[ \xymatrix{ X' \ar[d]_{f'} & \bar X' \ar[l]_{\pi'} \ar[dl]^{\bar f'} \\ S & } \] a diagram as in the statement, and \(U' \neq \emptyset\) an open of \(X'\) such that \(\pi'\) induces an isomorphism \(\bar U' = \pi'^{-1}(U') \to U'\). Since \(V\) is dense in \(X'\), \(U' \cap V \neq \emptyset\) and we can therefore replace \(U'\) with this open. Then \(U'\) is an open of \(X\), and we set \(\pi = i\pi'\), \(\bar f = \bar f'\), \(U = U'\).

But there exists a finite covering of \(X\) by non-affine open empty \(U_k\), \(k=1,\ldots,n\); from the hypothesis on \(S\) results that each \(U_k \to S\) is quasi-affine, and a fortiori quasi-compact. Let us prove the theorem for all \(X\), by induction on \(n\): If the intersection \(U\) of \(U_k\) is dense in \(X\) we can copy the demonstration of (EGA II 5.6, B, C, D), taking into account the modifications notation. If the intersection is non-empty, we return to the case where it is dense by replacing \(X\) by the adhesion \(X'\) of \(U\) (see above), and \(U_k\) by \(U'_k = U_k \cap X'\). Finally, suppose that \(U\) is empty and choose \(r\), \(1 \leq r \leq n\), such as \(V = \bigcap_{k=1}^{r} U_k \neq \emptyset\) but \(V \cap U_{r+1} = \emptyset\) — this is obviously possible since \(U_1 \neq \emptyset\). We can now replace \(X\) with the member- ence \(X'\) of \(V\) and the \(U\) by the non-empty \(U_k \cap V\). As then \(U_{r+1} \cap V\) is empty, we obtain the result by the recurrence hypothesis.

footnotes.html: (*) The reader who is not interested in the statements of par. 5 that in the Noetherian case can omit the reading of the present number.

blocks.html: 8. Permanent reductions

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.1

To prove one of the statements 5.1 (i) (ii) (iii), it is enough to do so when we assume \(f\) projective and \(S\) Noetherian.

Let's first assume 5.1 demonstrated for \(f\) projective, show how we conclude the same statement for all \(f\). Using 6.1, we are reduced to case of 5.5, with \(S\) strictly local. Let's now apply 6.10 by doing \(Y = X_o\). Given \((\xi,X')\) as stated in 6.10, we apply the lemma from Chow 7.1 to find a \(S\)-morphism \(p : \bar X \to X'\), with \(\bar X\) projective on \(S\), such that \(p(\bar X)\) contains a non-empty open of \(X'\) (condition 1°) of 6.10). Like \(p\) is projective, by assumption the base change homomorphisms for \((p,h)\) are isomorphisms (condition 2°) of 6.10). Finally, as \(X\) is projected tif on \(S\), by hypothesis the inclusion morphism \(\bar Y = \bar X_o \to \bar X\) induces an iso- morphism for cohomology groups \(H^i(\bar X, \bar F) \to H^i(\bar X_o, \bar h^*(\bar F))\), (condition 3°) of 6.10). So the conditions of 6.10 are well satisfied, hence the con- exclusion.

Let us now show that to verify one of the three statements 5.1 for any situation \((f,F,g)\), with \(f\) projective, we can further assume that \(S\) is Noetherian. For this, we note that we can assume thanks to 6.1 that \(S\) is strictly local and that \(S' \to S\) is the inclusion of the closed point, and we is reduced to verifying statement 5.5 with \(S\) strictly local. Moreover, as \(X\) immerses itself in the typical projective schema \(\mathbf{P}^r_S\), we can (thanks to the compati- bility 4.4 (ii)) replace \(X\) by \(\mathbf{P}^r_S\), \(F\) by its image in \(\mathbf{P}^r_S\), which gives us brings back to case \(X = \mathbf{P}^r_S\). Note that \(A\) is the filtering inductive limit of Noetherian Henselian local subrings \(A_i\) such as the inclusion homomorphism \(A_i \to A\) is local: take the strict henselizations of the sub-\(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\)-algebras of finite type of \(A\) into the prime ideals induced by the maximal ideal of \(A\). Under these conditions, the residual body \(k\) of \(A\) is also the inductive limit bodies \(k\) of \(A_i\) (EGA IV 5.13.1). Let \(S_i = \operatorname{Spec}(A_i)\), \(X_i = \mathbf{P}^r_{S_i}\), and suppose we have given an inductive system \((F_i)\) of bundles of sets (resp. ...) on the \(X_i\) as in VII 5.7, and let \(F\) be the beam it defines on \(X\). Either \(F_{io}\) (resp. \(F_o\)) the beam induced by \(F_i\) on the fiber \(X_{io}\) of the closed point \(s_i\) of \(S_i\) (resp. on the fiber \(X_o\) of the closed point \(s\) of \(S\)). So we have a diagram commutative \[ \xymatrix{ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_i, F_i) \ar[r] \ar[d] & H^n(X, F) \ar[d] \\ \varinjlim_i H^n(X_{io}, F_{io}) \ar[r] & H^n(X_o, F_o) , } \] whose two horizontal arrows are isomorphisms, by virtue of VII 5.7. Therefore, to prove that the second vertical arrow is an isomorph, it is enough to prove it for the first, so it is enough to prove that the homomorphisms \(H^n(X_i, F_i) \to H^n(X_{io}, F_{io})\) are isomorphisms. On the other hand, any sheaf of sets (resp. ...) \(F\) on \(X\) is isomorphic in the limit of a inductive system as above, taking for example \(F_i = u_{i*}(F)\), \(u_i : X \to X_i\) being the canonical morphism, by virtue of (5.2) and (IX 1.6 (iii)). This completes the demonstration of 8.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.2

Theorem 5.1 (i) is true.
In fact, it is enough to combine 8.1, criterion 6.5 (i) b''), and 5.8.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.3

To prove one of the statements 5.1 (i) (ii) (iii), simply do when we assume \(f\) projective of relative dimension \(\leq 1\), and \(S\) Noetherian.

Indeed, we already know from 8.1 that we can limit ourselves to the case \(f\) projective, \(S\) Noetherian, and an argument already mentioned allows us to suppose moreover we have \(X = \mathbf{P}^r_S\). We then proceed by induction on \(r\), noting that the theorem is true by hypothesis for \(r = 1\). We can therefore assume \(r \geq 2\), and the theorem proved for \(\mathbf{P}^{r-1}_S \to S\) (any basis \(S\)). Let's consider \(Z = \mathbf{P}^{r-2}_S\) as immersed in \(X = \mathbf{P}^r_S\) in the usual way, and either \(p : \bar X \to X\) obtained by exploding \(Z\) into \(X\). An immediate calculation (see EGA V) shows that we have a natural morphism \(p_1 : \bar X \to X_1 = \mathbf{P}^1_S\), which makes \(\bar X\) a \(X_1\)-schema locally isomorphic to \(\mathbf{P}^{r-1}_{X_1}\) (this is the projective bundle on \(X_1\) associated with a locally free beam of suitable rank \(r\) on \(X_1\)). Else On the other hand, the fibers of \(p\) are of dimension \(\leq 1\), as are those of \(f_1 : X_1 \to S\). We can therefore apply the hypothesis, and the hypothesis of recurrence, to the three morphisms \(p, p_1, f_1\) in the commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \bar X \ar[dl]_{p} \ar[dr]^{p_1} & \\ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & X_1 \ar[dl]^{f_1} \\ & S & } \] As \(p\) is surjective, Lemma 6.8 brings us back to prove 5.1 for the compound \(\bar f = fp = f_1 p_1\). Now we can already apply 5.1 to the two morphisms \(f_1\) and \(p_1\). Taking into account 6.11, we conclude that 5.1 is also true for their com- posed \(f_1 p_1\). This completes the demonstration of 8.3.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.4

To prove 5.1 (ii), it is enough to prove the "theorem of specialization for the fundamental group" 5.9 bis, in the case where \(f\) is projective of relative dimension \(\leq 1\), and where \(S\) is strictly local Noetherian.
This indeed follows immediately from 8.3, 6.1 and 6.5 (ii) (criterion b)).
We will directly demonstrate the specialization theorem, in the case 8.4, in the following presentation (XIII 2).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.5

To prove 5.1 (iii), it is enough to prove 5.1 (ii) and, moreover, the following proposition (which will be demonstrated in the following presentation (XIII 3) or (EGA IV 21.9.12)):

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.6

Let \(S\) Henselian and Noetherian, \(f : X \to S\) projective of relative dimension \(\leq 1\), and \(X_o\) the closed fiber of \(X/S\). For any schema \(Z\), or \(\operatorname{Pic} Z = H^1(Z, (\mathbb{G}_m)_Z)\) the Picard group of \(Z\). The restriction morphism \[ \operatorname{Pic} X \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pic} X_o \] is surjective.

Demonstration of 8.5. According to 6.5 (iii) and 8.2, it is sufficient to demonstrate that \[ \varphi^q : H^q(X, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z}) \longrightarrow H^q(X_o, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z}) \] is surjective for all \(q \geq 1\), and all prime numbers \(\ell\). But we know the surjectivity if \(q = 1\), by 5.1 (ii). Additionally, since \(X_o\) is a schema projective of dimension \(\leq 1\) on a body \(k\) separably closed, we have \[ H^q(X_o, \mathbb{Z}/\ell^\nu \mathbb{Z}) = 0 \] if \(q > 2\) in the case \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\) (X 4.3), and if \(q > 1\) in the case \(\ell = \operatorname{car} k\) (X 5.2). Surjectivity is therefore trivial for these values ​​of \(q\)!

It remains to process the case \(q = 2\) and \(\ell \neq \operatorname{car} k\). Let us note \(n = \ell^\nu\). As the bundle of the \(n\)-th roots of the unit \((\mu_n)_S\) is locally iso- morph to \((\mathbb{Z}/n)_S\), and \(S\) is strictly local, there exists an isomorphism (non-canonical) \((\mu_n)_S \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_S\) hence \((\mu_n)_X \simeq (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_X\).

By Kummer IX 3.2 theory applied to \(X\) and \(X_o\), we have a morphism of exact sequences \[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Pic} X \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\varepsilon} & H^2(X, \mu_n) \ar[r] \ar[d]^{\varphi^2} & H^2(X, \mathbb{G}_m) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Pic} X_o \ar[r] & H^2(X_o, \mu_n) \ar[r] & H^2(X_o, \mathbb{G}_m) . } \] Or \(H^2(X_o, \mathbb{G}_m) = 0\) according to IX 4.6. Therefore the surjectivity of \(\varepsilon\) implies that of \(\varphi^2\), hence the lemma.
